title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 6 (Fragment 3)
text:
Benvenuto gave a whisk of his tail and leaped for the gallery steps. He bounded up them, followed by the other cats; and Paolo and Rosa hurried up too, in a sort of shoal of leaping black and white bodies. Everyone converged on Antonio’s rooms. People poured out of the Scriptorium, Elizabeth raced around the gallery, and Aunt Maria and Aunt Gina clambered up the steps by the kitchen quicker than either had ever climbed in her life. The Casa filled with the sound of hollow running feet.

The whole family jammed themselves after Rosa and Paolo into the room where Tonino was usually to be found reading. There was no Tonino, only the red book lying on the windowsill. It was no longer shiny. The pages were thick at the edges and the red cover was curling upwards, as if the book was wet.

Benvenuto, with his jagged brown coat up in a ridge along his back and his tail fluffed like a fox’s brush, landed on the sill beside the book and rashly put his nose forward to sniff at it. He leaped back again, shaking his head, crouching, and growling like a dog. Smoke poured up from the book. People coughed and cats sneezed. The book curled and writhed on the sill, amid clouds of smoke, exactly as if it were on fire. But instead of turning black, it turned pale gray-blue where it smoked, and looked slimy. The room filled with a smell of rotting.

“Ugh!” said everybody.

Old Niccolo barged members of his family right and left to get near it. He stood over it and sang, in a strong tenor voice almost as good as Marco’s, three strange words. He sang them twice before he had to break off coughing. “Sing!” he croaked, with tears pouring down his face. “All of you.”

All the Montanas obediently broke into song, three long notes in unison. And again. And again. After that, quite a number of them had to cough, though the smoke was distinctly less. Old Niccolo recovered and waved his arms, like the conductor of a choir. All who could, sang once more. It took ten repetitions to halt the decay of the book. By that time, it was a shriveled triangle, about half the size it had been. Gingerly, Antonio leaned over and opened the window beyond it, to let out the last of the smoke.

“What was it?” he asked Old Niccolo. “Some one trying to suffocate us all?”

“I thought it came from Umberto,” Elizabeth faltered. “I never would have—”

Old Niccolo shook his head. “This thing never came from Umberto. And I don’t think it was meant to kill. Let’s see what kind of spell it is.” He snapped his fingers and held out a hand, rather like a surgeon performing an operation. Without needing to be told, Aunt Gina put her kitchen tongs into his hand. Carefully, gently, Old Niccolo used the tongs to open the cover of the book.

“A good pair of tongs ruined,” Aunt Gina said.

“Ssh!” said Old Niccolo. The shriveled pages of the book had stuck into a gummy block. He snapped his fingers and held out his hand again. This time, Rinaldo put the pen he was carrying in it.

“And a good pen,” he said, with a grimace at Aunt Gina.

With the pen as well as the tongs, Old Niccolo was able to pry the pages of the book apart without touching them and peel them over, one by one. Chins rested on both Paolo’s shoulders as everyone craned to see, and there were chins on the shoulders of those with the chins. There was no sound but the sound of breathing.

vocabulary.definition: To push or force one's way roughly or abruptly.
vocabulary.example: Old Niccolo barged members of his family right and left to get near it.

vocabulary.definition: To leap or jump quickly or energetically.
vocabulary.example: He bounded up them, followed by the other cats; and Paolo and Rosa hurried up too, in a sort of shoal of leaping black and white bodies.

vocabulary.definition: To come together from different directions to meet at a common point.
vocabulary.example: Everyone converged on Antonio’s rooms.

vocabulary.definition: To stretch out one's neck to see better.
vocabulary.example: Chins rested on both Paolo’s shoulders as everyone craned to see, and there were chins on the shoulders of those with the chins.

vocabulary.definition: To speak or make a rough, hoarse sound.
vocabulary.example: ” he croaked, with tears pouring down his face.

vocabulary.definition: To bend the knees and lower the body close to the ground.
vocabulary.example: He leaped back again, shaking his head, crouching, and growling like a dog.

vocabulary.definition: Forming into a curved or spiral shape.
vocabulary.example: The pages were thick at the edges and the red cover was curling upwards, as if the book was wet.

vocabulary.definition: The process of declining in quality, power, or condition; decomposition.
vocabulary.example: It took ten repetitions to halt the decay of the book.

vocabulary.definition: To speak hesitantly or lose strength or momentum.
vocabulary.example:
”

“I thought it came from Umberto,” Elizabeth faltered.

vocabulary.definition: To make something, especially hair or fur, look fuller and softer by shaking or fluffing it.
vocabulary.example: Benvenuto, with his jagged brown coat up in a ridge along his back and his tail fluffed like a fox’s brush, landed on the sill beside the book and rashly put his nose forward to sniff at it.

vocabulary.definition: A facial expression usually of pain, disgust, or disapproval.
vocabulary.example: “And a good pen,” he said, with a grimace at Aunt Gina.

vocabulary.definition: To make a low, guttural sound, typically showing anger or warning.
vocabulary.example: He leaped back again, shaking his head, crouching, and growling like a dog.

vocabulary.definition: Having sharp, uneven points or edges.
vocabulary.example: Benvenuto, with his jagged brown coat up in a ridge along his back and his tail fluffed like a fox’s brush, landed on the sill beside the book and rashly put his nose forward to sniff at it.

vocabulary.definition: To open or separate with force or difficulty.
vocabulary.example: With the pen as well as the tongs, Old Niccolo was able to pry the pages of the book apart without touching them and peel them over, one by one.

vocabulary.definition: Acting hastily or without careful consideration.
vocabulary.example: Benvenuto, with his jagged brown coat up in a ridge along his back and his tail fluffed like a fox’s brush, landed on the sill beside the book and rashly put his nose forward to sniff at it.

vocabulary.definition: A long narrow raised part of a surface, especially on the back of an animal.
vocabulary.example: Benvenuto, with his jagged brown coat up in a ridge along his back and his tail fluffed like a fox’s brush, landed on the sill beside the book and rashly put his nose forward to sniff at it.

vocabulary.definition: Decaying or decomposing organic matter.
vocabulary.example: The room filled with a smell of rotting.

vocabulary.definition: Reflecting light easily; bright and smooth.
vocabulary.example: It was no longer shiny.

vocabulary.definition: Wrinkled and shrunken, typically due to loss of moisture or aging.
vocabulary.example: By that time, it was a shriveled triangle, about half the size it had been.

vocabulary.definition: To become wrinkled or shrunken, especially due to loss of moisture.
vocabulary.example: By that time, it was a shriveled triangle, about half the size it had been.

vocabulary.definition: Covered with or resembling slime; slippery and unpleasant.
vocabulary.example: But instead of turning black, it turned pale gray-blue where it smoked, and looked slimy.

vocabulary.definition: To cause to die or lose consciousness by preventing access to air.
vocabulary.example: “Some one trying to suffocate us all?

vocabulary.definition: The highest male singing voice; a type of vocal range.
vocabulary.example: He stood over it and sang, in a strong tenor voice almost as good as Marco’s, three strange words.

vocabulary.definition: A quick, light movement or action, especially of a tail or similar part.
vocabulary.example: Benvenuto gave a whisk of his tail and leaped for the gallery steps.

vocabulary.definition: To twist the body about violently, especially in pain or discomfort.
vocabulary.example: The book curled and writhed on the sill, amid clouds of smoke, exactly as if it were on fire.
